title: Catechist's Guide — The Holy Trinity
html:
Catechist's Guide

For class preparation and delivery. For the catechist's use only.

Class Summary

Estimated duration: 75 minutes

Central theme: God reveals himself as Trinity — one God in three Persons — and this mystery is not an abstract doctrine but the heart of the Christian faith, the pattern of our salvation, and the home into which we are invited to enter.

Objectives:

  • Understand that the Trinity is "the central mystery of the Christian faith" (CCC 234), not an advanced or secondary topic, but the foundation of every other doctrine.
  • Trace how God progressively revealed himself as Trinity: Old Testament glimpses, full revelation in Jesus Christ, apostolic understanding after the Resurrection.
  • Distinguish that the three divine Persons are really distinct — each is fully God — yet share the same single divine substance (homoousios).
  • Internalize that "God is Love" (1 Jn 4:8, 16) is the key that unlocks the entire doctrine of the Trinity: God is not and has never been solitude.
  • Connect Trinitarian theology to daily Christian living: prayer, liturgy, relationships, moral life (Beatitudes, chastity), and the indwelling of the Trinity in the soul through grace.

Key verse: "The grace of the Lord Jesus Christ, the love of God, and the fellowship of the Holy Spirit be with you all" (2 Cor 13:13)

CCC references: 232-267

Scripture references: Mt 28:19; 2 Cor 13:13; Jn 14:16-17; Mt 3:16-17; Jn 1:1-3; 1 Jn 4:8

Section 1 — Suggested Class Structure

Section Duration What the catechist does
1. Opening and Big Question 8 min Begin with the Sign of the Cross — slowly, naming each Person. Pose the Big Question: "How can God be three and one at the same time?" Listen to spontaneous answers. Do not try to resolve the mystery; awaken curiosity. Introduce Rublev's icon of the Trinity and the empty seat — the Trinity has room for you.
2. The Central Mystery of the Faith 7 min Explain that the Trinity is not an add-on but the heart of everything. It is "the most fundamental and essential teaching in the hierarchy of the truths of faith" (CCC 234). Clarify that "mystery" does not mean irrational — it means infinitely knowable but inexhaustible. Activity: make the Sign of the Cross slowly, noticing "in the name" (singular, not plural).
3. Progressive Revelation 10 min Walk through salvation history: Shema (Dt 6:4) → Old Testament glimpses (Wisdom, Spirit hovering over waters, Servant of the Lord) → Jesus reveals the Father uniquely ("The Father and I are one," Jn 10:30) → Promise of the Holy Spirit (Jn 14:16-17) → Apostolic understanding after Resurrection. Read together: Gen 1:1-2 and Jn 1:1-3.
4. The Three Divine Persons and the Council of Nicaea 12 min Explain that the Persons are distinguished by relations, not by nature. What distinguishes them is not what they are (all are God) but how they relate to one another. Tell the story of Arius and the Council of Nicaea: if the Son is not truly God, then God did not save us. The word homoousios (consubstantial) preserved the Gospel. Read Mt 28:19: the singular "name" for three Persons.
5. God Is a Communion of Love 10 min Use 1 Jn 4:8 as the central key: God IS love — not "has" love. Love requires Lover, Beloved, and the Love between them. The Father is the Lover, the Son is the Beloved, the Holy Spirit is the Love between them. Tell the story of St. Augustine and the child on the beach. God has never been alone; He is an eternal family of love.
6. Analogies: Their Power and Limits 8 min Review the analogies: St. Patrick's shamrock, St. Augustine's lover-beloved-love, family as icon of the Trinity, C.S. Lewis on prayer. For each: what it captures, and where it falls short. Emphasize that every analogy of the Trinity breaks if pressed too far — this is not a failure of the analogies but a testament to the uniqueness of God.
7. Trinitarian Missions and Our Lives 8 min The circle of love: from the Father, through the Son, in the Spirit, to us, back to the Father. Connection with prayer, liturgy, and daily life. Introduce the indwelling of the Trinity in the soul through sanctifying grace. Connect with confession and mortal sin as expelling the Trinity.
8. Biblical Connection: The Baptism of Jesus 6 min Read Mt 3:16-17 aloud. Point out the three Persons manifest simultaneously. Ask: "What does this scene tell us about who God is? And what does your own baptism mean now that you see it as a Trinitarian event?"
9. For Reflection and Closing 6 min Choose 2-3 reflection questions for small group sharing, then brief plenary. Close with the Glory Be prayed slowly and the Blessing from 2 Cor 13:13.

Section 2 — Key Theological Notes for the Catechist

The Mystery Is Not Irrational

Before you teach anything else, clarify this. In everyday English, "mystery" means a puzzle — something to be solved. In Christian theology, "mystery" means something very different: a reality so deep, so rich, so infinite that we can know it truly (because God has revealed it) but never exhaust it. St. Augustine understood this correctly: it is not that we know nothing about the Trinity, but that there is always more to know. Analogies like the shamrock or water serve as starting points, but they are always limited. The important thing is not to "explain" the Trinity but to enter into it. As the Catechism says: "The Trinity is a mystery of faith in the strict sense, one of the 'mysteries that are hidden in God, which can never be known unless they are revealed by God'" (CCC 237).

The Heresy of Arius: Why Nicaea Mattered

The Council of Nicaea (325) was not an irrelevant academic dispute about Greek vocabulary. If the Son is not true God — if he is a creature, however exalted — then on the Cross, it was not God who saved us, but a created intermediary. A creature cannot bridge the infinite distance between fallen humanity and the living God. Only God can save. The word homoousios (consubstantial, "of the same substance") was chosen deliberately: the Son does not merely resemble the Father; he is of the same being. An analogy you can use: if someone is saving you from drowning, you do not want them to send a representative; you want them to come personally. That is what God did in Christ. "The Church confesses that Jesus is inseparably true God and true man. He is truly the Son of God who, without ceasing to be God and Lord, became a man and our brother" (CCC 469).

"God Is Love" — The Most Important Sentence in the Entire Lesson

1 John 4:8, 16 is the keystone of all Trinitarian theology. If the participants take away only one thing, let it be this: God is not alone. He has never been alone. He is, from all eternity and forever, a communion of love. The Father loves the Son with infinite and eternal love, and that love is so real, so substantial, that it is a divine Person — the Holy Spirit. This radically changes our image of God. He is not a solitary, distant being contemplating himself; he is a family of love that invites us to enter. The Catechism states: "By no way of reasoning can man arrive at the mystery of the Trinity. Nevertheless, God has left traces of his Trinitarian being in his work of creation and in his Revelation throughout the Old Testament. But his inmost Being as Holy Trinity is a mystery that is inaccessible to reason alone or even to Israel's faith before the Incarnation of God's Son and the sending of the Holy Spirit" (CCC 237).

The Divine Relations: Not Three "Roles" but Three Persons

What distinguishes the Persons is not what they are (all three are God — fully, equally, eternally) but the eternal relationships between them. The Father generates (he is the one who begets); the Son is begotten (not made, not created); the Holy Spirit proceeds (from the Father and the Son). They are not three "roles" that God assumes sequentially — that is modalism, a heresy. They are three truly distinct Persons who share the same divine nature. The distinction is relational and only relational. You can illustrate this by comparing it to human personhood: a person is constituted by relationship. But the divine Persons are perfectly relational: to be the Father is to be in relation to the Son; to be the Son is to be in relation to the Father; to be the Spirit is to be the bond between them. Each Person is fully God, yet the three are not three gods but one God — because there is only one divine substance, and the three Persons share it entirely.

The Trinitarian Missions and the Opera ad Extra

Every external work of God (opera ad extra) is the work of all three Persons together. Creation is not the Father's alone but the Father creating through the Word in the Holy Spirit. Redemption is not the Son's alone but the Father sending the Son and the Spirit resting upon him. Sanctification is not the Spirit's alone but the Spirit sent by the Father and the Son. Yet the tradition appropriately "attributes" certain works to the Person whose personal property is most clearly manifested: creation to the Father (origin without origin), redemption to the Son (incarnate and visible), sanctification to the Holy Spirit (indwelling gift and fire). There is no competition in the Trinity, only communion — each Person glorifying the others, each giving everything away, each receiving everything back.

The Liturgy Is Trinitarian — and That Changes Everything

Every Mass, every sacrament, every Christian prayer is Trinitarian by structure. We do not pray only to the Father, or only to the Son, or only to the Holy Spirit. All Christian prayer goes through the Son, in the Holy Spirit, to the Father. The Catechism teaches: "The liturgy is also a participation in Christ's own prayer addressed to the Father in the Holy Spirit. In the liturgy, all Christian prayer finds its source and goal" (CCC 1073). If someone asks "what is this good for in practical life?" the answer is: every time you pray, every time you receive a sacrament, you are entering into the life of the Trinity. There is nothing more practical than that. The Trinity is not a distant truth; it is the atmosphere we breathe in every liturgical action.

Section 3 — The Narrative: St. Augustine and the Child on the Beach

This story is the emotional and imaginative heart of the lesson. Tell it, do not just summarize it. Set the scene: the burning North African sun, the Mediterranean waves, the great bishop walking alone with his thoughts. He has been trying for years to write his book De Trinitate (On the Trinity) — a work that would take him twenty years and would remain, in his own judgment, incomplete. The weight of the mystery is heavy on him. He sees a child — small, absorbed, determined — running back and forth from the sea to a hole in the sand, carrying water in a shell. Run, pour, run, pour. Augustine asks the question any adult would ask: "What are you doing?" And the child's answer — "I am putting the whole ocean into this hole" — brings forth the smile of the worldly-wise: "But that is impossible."

Then comes the reversal. The child looks at Augustine with eyes that are not a child's eyes, and says: "It is even more impossible for you to fit the mystery of the Trinity into your mind." And he vanishes. Augustine is left standing in the surf. He has been taught, by a child who was no child, the most important lesson of Trinitarian theology: the Trinity is not a puzzle to be solved but a Person to be loved, a communion to be entered. The right posture before the Trinity is not the theologian taking notes but the worshipper on his knees.

Why this story matters for your class: Your students — especially adults — may come with the assumption that they need to "understand" the Trinity intellectually before they can believe it. This story gently dismantles that assumption. Understanding is good — the Church has always pursued it — but it must end in adoration, not in a closed system of concepts. C.S. Lewis makes the same point: "The thing that matters is being actually drawn into that three-personal life." The child on the beach is an icon of humility: the mystery is bigger than us, and that is a feature, not a bug.

Section 4 — Cultural Resources: Using Art and Story

Rublev's Icon of the Trinity (1411)

If you do only one thing to prepare for this class beyond reading the lesson, find a high-quality digital image of Rublev's icon and either project it on a screen or print it large enough to see details. Walk the class through it:

  • The three angels: Identical faces, distinct postures. The Father (left, in gold robe, looking toward the Son); the Son (center, in red tunic — the color of sacrifice — and blue mantle, fingers blessing the chalice); the Holy Spirit (right, in green — the color of life and renewal — bowing toward the offering).
  • The chalice: At the exact center of the table and the icon. Inside: the head of a lamb. The Eucharist and the Cross occupy the center of Trinitarian life. The love of the Trinity is sacrificial love.
  • The three background symbols: The house (Father's dwelling, the Church, the heavenly mansion); the tree (the Oak of Mamre — and the Tree of the Cross); the mountain (the place of encounter with God, the ascent to which the Spirit draws us).
  • The empty space: At the front of the table, facing the viewer. Rublev deliberately left an opening. The Trinity is not a closed circle; it has a place prepared for each person. You are invited to sit at this table.

St. Patrick's Shamrock (5th Century)

The shamrock is simple to grasp and has deep cultural resonance. But be careful to explain its limits immediately: each leaf is only a part of the shamrock — one-third of the plant — but each divine Person is fully, completely God, not one-third of God. Use the shamrock as a starting point, then move beyond it. The real importance of St. Patrick is not the shamrock; it is the fact that a former slave returned to evangelize his captors — a living image of Trinitarian forgiveness.

C.S. Lewis, Mere Christianity (1952)

Lewis's chapter on the Trinity is a model of how to teach this doctrine to intelligent adults who lack theological formation. He avoids every technical term and instead describes what it feels like to be a Trinitarian Christian at prayer: God outside you prompting you to pray, God inside you helping you to pray, and God beside you (Christ in his humanity) showing you how. Read a paragraph aloud in class — it will communicate more than any technical explanation.

Montserrat's Trinity Chapel

The Benedictine monastery of Montserrat in Catalonia, perched among jagged peaks, has been a pilgrimage site for nearly a thousand years. Its Trinity Chapel is not a famous artwork but a living shrine — a reminder that the Trinity is encountered on pilgrimage, not just in lecture halls. If you have members of the class who have visited Montserrat or other pilgrimage sites, invite them to share their experience of approaching a holy place. The journey toward the Trinity costs something — and that is part of its power.

Bl. Chiara Badano (Chiara Luce)

A contemporary witness. Chiara was a normal Italian teenager — she loved tennis, swimming, pop music, and her friends. Diagnosed with aggressive bone cancer at seventeen, she transformed her hospital room into a Trinitarian place of welcome. She refused morphine not because she wanted to suffer, but because she wanted to offer her suffering with full consciousness: "I have nothing left," she said, "but I still have my heart, and with that I can always love." Her last words to her mother: "Be happy, because I am happy. Ciao, Mamma." She was beatified in 2010 at age 25. Chiara Luce makes the Trinity accessible to young people who might find theological language alien: the Trinity is lived, not just discussed.

Section 5 — Age Adaptation

🌱 Seed (ages 7-12)

Approach: Concrete, sensory, story-driven. Avoid all theological vocabulary except "Father," "Son," and "Holy Spirit."

  • Start with the Sign of the Cross: teach them to make it slowly and name each Person. This is their first Trinitarian act of worship.
  • Use St. Patrick's shamrock as the simplest starting point, but immediately say: "But each leaf is only part of the clover — and God the Father, God the Son, and God the Holy Spirit are each completely God, not just parts."
  • Draw the baptism of Jesus together (Mt 3:16-17): Jesus in the water, the dove above him, and a cloud with a voice. Label each: "Son," "Holy Spirit," "Father." That is the Trinity made visible.
  • Teach 2 Cor 13:13 with gestures: hands open for "grace," hand on heart for "love," arms out for "fellowship."
  • Memorization activity: each child says one sentence of the Glory Be, passing it around the circle.
  • Avoid: processions, relations, substance, homoousios, opera ad extra.

🌿 Sprouts (ages 13-17)

Approach: Connect Trinitarian theology to their hunger for authentic relationships. Teenagers are discovering that love matters — and that love can be distorted. The Trinity offers the original pattern.

  • Opening question: "Do you feel more alive when you are giving yourself to someone you love, or when you are protecting yourself from being hurt?" Let them answer honestly.
  • Rublev's icon is powerful for this age: the circle, the empty space, the invitation. "Why do you think Rublev left an empty seat?"
  • Connect the Trinity to friendship, dating, and family: "If God is a communion of Persons who give themselves away, what does that mean for how you love someone? Do you use people or do you give yourself to them?"
  • C.S. Lewis's description of Trinitarian prayer — God outside, inside, and beside — is concrete and relatable.
  • Chiara Badano (Chiara Luce) is a saint for them: young, athletic, normal, and radiant with Trinitarian love even as she was dying.
  • Difficult question to expect: "So is God like a family with three members?" Answer: Yes — and no. Yes, because the Church calls the Trinity a communion of Persons and compares it to a family. No, because the three Persons are not three separate beings; they share the one and only divine nature perfectly.

🪴 Root (adults new to the faith)

Approach: Start from experience, not from doctrine. Most adults in RCIA or returning to the faith already have some intuition of Trinitarian life: they have felt loved unconditionally by someone, or known the peace of prayer, or experienced a moment of grace. Name these experiences and then show how the Trinity explains them.

  • Opening: "Have you ever felt that God loves you as a Father — origin, protector, provider? That Jesus is present beside you — companion, friend, savior? That the Holy Spirit gives you strength, peace, or courage when you need it? Those three experiences are Trinitarian life in practice."
  • Explain progressive revelation slowly: God prepared humanity for the Trinity across centuries. The Jews knew God is one; Jesus revealed God is also three. Both are true; neither cancels the other.
  • Focus on 1 Jn 4:8 as the interpretive key: God is love. Love is relational. Therefore the one God must be interpersonal at his core. This is the simplest and deepest argument for the Trinity, and it requires no philosophical training to grasp.
  • Spend time on the indwelling of the Trinity in the soul through grace. For newly baptized or newly returned adults, the idea that God lives inside them is both astonishing and deeply consoling.
  • Avoid: diving into the Arian controversy in depth, the Filioque debate, or technical language about processions. Save those for the 🌳 level.

🌳 Tree (formed adults)

Approach: Full theological depth. These participants have the doctrinal foundation to appreciate the precision of Trinitarian theology and the beauty of its development.

  • Explore the divine processions in depth: the generation of the Son (eternal begetting, not creation) and the spiration of the Holy Spirit (from the Father and the Son as from one principle). Distinguish between the immanent Trinity (God in himself) and the economic Trinity (God as revealed in salvation history), while affirming they are the same Trinity.
  • Walk through the Arian controversy in detail, including the Council of Constantinople (381) that completed the Creed with the article on the Holy Spirit: "the Lord, the giver of life, who proceeds from the Father and the Son, who with the Father and the Son is adored and glorified."
  • Introduce the Filioque question and the distinction between Eastern and Western approaches to the Trinity — with pastoral sensitivity, not polemics.
  • Read the Cappadocian Fathers: St. Basil the Great, St. Gregory of Nyssa, and St. Gregory Nazianzen, who gave the Church the precise Trinitarian vocabulary — one ousia (substance), three hypostaseis (persons).
  • Study St. Augustine's De Trinitate and its psychological analogy (memory, understanding, will as an image of the Trinity in the human soul), with Augustine's own caveat that these analogies are partial and must end in adoration.
  • Discuss St. Thomas Aquinas, Summa Theologiae I, qq. 27-43: the divine relations as subsistent — what makes the Persons distinct is pure relationality. The Person, in God, is not something behind or underneath the relation; the Person is the subsistent relation.
  • Connect Trinitarian theology to ecclesiology: the Church as an icon of the Trinity (Lumen Gentium 2-4), and to anthropology: the human person made in the image of the Trinitarian God finds fulfillment in self-gift (Gaudium et Spes 24).
  • Engage with modern Trinitarian theology: Karl Rahner's axiom ("The economic Trinity is the immanent Trinity, and the immanent Trinity is the economic Trinity"), Hans Urs von Balthasar's Theo-Drama, and Joseph Ratzinger's Trinitarian Christology.

Section 6 — Supplementary Resources

  • YouCat (Youth Catechism): Questions 35-38 ("What does it mean that God is Trinity?", "Are the three divine Persons different?", "How do we know that God is Trinity?", "Is the Trinity a logical contradiction?"). Excellent for teen and young adult preparation.
  • CCC Compendium: Questions 44-49 — a concise summary of the Trinitarian doctrine in question-and-answer format. Useful as a handout.
  • Opus Dei: Topic "God One and Triune" in the "Summaries of Catholic Teaching" series at opusdei.org. Clear, systematic, and freely available.
  • Video: Bishop Robert Barron's "The Trinity" (Word on Fire) — a brief and engaging introduction to Trinitarian theology. Also: "The Baptism of Jesus" clips from The Chosen or other biblical film productions showing the Trinitarian epiphany.
  • Art: High-resolution image of Rublev's Trinity icon. The Tretyakov Gallery in Moscow has published high-quality digital reproductions. Consider also: El Greco's "The Holy Trinity" (1577, Prado Museum), Masaccio's "The Holy Trinity" fresco in Santa Maria Novella, Florence — the first painting to use linear perspective, and a powerful Trinitarian composition.
  • Books: Joseph Ratzinger (Benedict XVI), Introduction to Christianity — the chapter on the Trinity is one of the finest introductions available. Frank Sheed, Theology for Beginners — chapter on the Trinity is accessible and profound. Gilles Emery, OP, The Trinity: An Introduction to Catholic Doctrine on the Triune God — for the 🌳 level.
  • Music: Hakuna Group Music, "Dios Trinity" (contemporary Spanish worship); the Te Deum (traditional hymn of praise to the Trinity); John Michael Talbot, "Come Worship the Lord" (Trinitarian themes).

Section 7 — Difficult Questions and How to Answer Them

"Isn't the Trinity a logical contradiction? 3 = 1?"

It is not a contradiction because we are not saying that three Persons are one Person, or that three gods are one God. We are saying that three Persons share one divine nature. Contradiction would be: "God is three Persons and God is one Person in the same way at the same time." That is not the Christian claim. The Christian claim is that God is three Persons in one substance. The "three" refers to the Persons; the "one" refers to the divine nature. These are different aspects of the same reality. A helpful analogy: in a human family, each person is a distinct "who," but all share the same human nature. In God, the nature is not multiplied; the three Persons share the one and only divine nature perfectly and entirely. This is not a fully satisfactory analogy — no analogy is — but it clarifies the logic. The Trinity is not a mathematical error; it is a truth that surpasses mathematics (CCC 252-255).

"Why are Christians monotheists if they believe in three gods?"

This question is especially common in interreligious dialogue with Jews and Muslims, and it deserves a respectful, clear answer. Christians believe in one God — absolutely and without compromise. The Shema ("Hear, O Israel, the Lord our God, the Lord is One") is as binding on Christians as on Jews. The Catechism states it unambiguously: "We do not confess three gods, but one God in three Persons, the 'consubstantial Trinity.' The divine Persons do not share the one divinity among themselves but each of them is God whole and entire" (CCC 253). The three Persons are not three separate gods because they share the same single divine essence, the same will, the same operation, the same glory. They are distinguished only by their relations — not by being, not by nature. The Church insists on monotheism so strongly that every major Trinitarian heresy was an attempt to protect the oneness of God — but the right way to protect it is not to deny the threeness (Arianism, modalism) but to affirm the right relationship between the one nature and the three Persons.

"Aren't the analogies — shamrock, water, sun — heretical?"

Every analogy of the Trinity is limited, and if taken too far, every analogy can fall into heresy. The shamrock (three leaves, one plant) can suggest that each Person is only a "part" of God — partialism, which is heretical. Water (ice, liquid, steam) is modalism — one substance appearing in three different forms or modes. The sun (disc, light, heat) comes closer but can suggest Arianism — that the Son and Spirit are emanations or creations from the Father. St. Augustine's psychological analogy (memory, understanding, will) is more sophisticated but still only images one Person with three faculties, not three Persons. The key is this: use analogies as clues, not as explanations. They point toward the mystery; they do not contain it. Warn your students that every analogy breaks, and that the break is itself instructive — it shows the uniqueness of God. As the Catechism says: "The real distinction of the persons from one another resides solely in the relationships which relate them to one another" (CCC 255). No analogy in creation perfectly captures a being whose persons are constituted entirely by relation.

"If the three Persons act together, why do we say the Father creates, the Son redeems, the Spirit sanctifies?"

The external works of God (opera ad extra) are the common work of all three Persons. When the Father creates, He creates through the Son (the Word) in the Holy Spirit. When the Son redeems, He is sent by the Father and anointed by the Spirit. When the Spirit sanctifies, He is sent by the Father and the Son. The three never act separately. However, the tradition appropriately "attributes" certain works to the Person whose personal property is most clearly revealed in that work: creation to the Father (the origin without origin, the source of all being), redemption to the Son (who became incarnate, visible, and tangible), sanctification to the Holy Spirit (who is the indwelling gift, the interior fire). These attributions are theological language at the service of worship; they are not descriptions of internal divisions in God's action. God always acts as one, and the one divine action is always the action of all three Persons together (CCC 257-260).

"Why does it matter whether I believe in the Trinity? Can't I just believe in God?"

This question often comes from a sincere place — the sense that doctrine divides while love unites. The answer must be given with gentleness and clarity. The Trinity is not an optional belief added to a generic "God" — it is the revelation of who God actually is. Jesus did not say "Believe in a generic supreme being." He said: "The Father and I are one" (Jn 10:30) and "I will send you the Holy Spirit" (Jn 16:7). If Jesus is God — and He claimed to be — then God is Trinity. There is no way around this. To reject the Trinity is to reject what Jesus revealed about himself. But the pastoral approach matters: many people who ask this question have been wounded by religious conflict or taught that Christianity is about passing doctrinal exams. The invitation is not "believe this formula or else" but "come and see — this is who God has shown himself to be, and this is the love into which you are invited." The Trinity is the shape of salvation. If you do not believe in the Trinity, you do not yet know the God whom Jesus came to reveal — but Jesus is patient, and the Holy Spirit is gentle, and the Father's house has many rooms. The response to this question should open a door, not close one (CCC 234, 243).

Section 8 — Catechist's Prayer

Glory to the Father, and to the Son, and to the Holy Spirit,
God, one and triune,
who has called me, unworthy as I am,
to speak of your greatest mystery — the mystery of who You are.

Give me simple words for an immense mystery,
a heart that burns with your Trinitarian love,
and the humility to remember
that I do not explain the Trinity —
I adore it, I love it, and I live it.

Father, source of all being:
let my words flow from you and return to you.
Son, Word made flesh:
speak through my poor human speech.
Holy Spirit, fire of love:
set these words alight in the hearts of those who hear them.

May those I teach not remain in ideas,
but encounter You —
the communion of love that preceded all worlds,
the communion of love that redeemed a fallen world,
the communion of love that awaits them at the end of all things.

And when the class ends and the words fade,
may they find themselves — without quite knowing how —
sitting at the table you have prepared,
in the seat you have left open,
held in the love that has no end.
Amen.

Print Catechist's Guide
